negative_variants.description: Current Contribution Conductance: uses absolute V(p) instead of branch voltage V(p,n), so n=0 smoke tests can pass but differential-bias checks fail.
Current Contribution Conductance: only contributes current for positive branch voltage, so positive-only smoke tests can pass but negative V(p,n) hidden checks fail.
Current Contribution Conductance: adds a small DC current offset, so coarse shape checks can pass while calibrated zero-bias and gain checks fail.
Current Contribution Conductance: contributes through the reversed branch direction, preserving magnitude but failing signed current checks.
Current Contribution Conductance: uses half the required conductance, so simple sign checks pass but calibrated current samples fail.
